---
editor:
    render-on-save: true
csl: ieee.csl
format:
  html:
    filters: [tikz.lua, strip-yaml-frontmatter.lua, maintext-filter.lua, sitext-filter.lua]
---

## Robustness of CLR Inference to Capture Probability Misspecification {#sec-diffexp-robustness}

A natural concern with any compositional analysis framework is its sensitivity
to upstream modelling assumptions. The empirical CLR differential expression
pipeline developed in the preceding sections operates entirely in compositional
(CLR) space, where each gene's effect is measured relative to the geometric mean
of all genes. A natural question is: what happens to CLR-based inference when
the capture probability $\nu_c$ is not estimated correctly?

As discussed in @sec-cap-identifiability, the observed library size constrains
only the product $\nu_c \cdot \mu_T$, and resolving this degeneracy requires
external information (the biology-informed prior on total mRNA,
@sec-capture-prior). If that anchor is imperfect, one might worry that all
downstream inferences are compromised.

In this section, we show that CLR-based differential expression is exactly
invariant to capture probability misspecification. The argument rests on a
single structural property of the generative model: binomial thinning preserves
the shape parameter of the negative binomial distribution, and the compositional
distribution depends on these shape parameters in a way that is immune to the
capture scaling. This exact invariance holds under both the shared-$p$ model and
the gene-specific $p_g$ generalization, and extends to mixture models where
different cell types have different total mRNA contents.

In contrast, the biological-level metrics developed in the next section absorb
the capture scaling error directly and lose their absolute interpretation. This
asymmetry reinforces the recommended workflow: use CLR as the primary screen,
then validate with biological metrics.

#### Binomial thinning preserves the NB shape parameter

Recall from @sec-dirichlet-multinomial the Poisson--Gamma mixture representation
of the negative binomial. For each gene $g$, the true (pre-capture) transcript
count is generated as

$$
\lambda_g \sim \text{Gamma}(r_g, \beta), \qquad
m_g \mid \lambda_g \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_g),
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-poisson-gamma}

where $r_g$ is the gene-specific shape (dispersion) parameter and $\beta$ is a
rate parameter shared across genes (determined by $p$ or $\phi$). Marginally,
$m_g \sim \text{NB}(r_g, p)$, and the conditional distribution of normalized
expression is $\underline{\rho} \mid \underline{r} \sim
\text{Dirichlet}(\underline{r})$ (@eq-norm-dirichlet-conditional).

Under the variable capture probability model, the observed UMI count $u_g$ is
obtained by binomially thinning $m_g$ with probability $\nu_c$:

$$
u_g \mid m_g, \nu_c \sim \text{Binomial}(m_g, \nu_c).
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-thinning}

Conditional on the latent Gamma rate, this thinning simply rescales the Poisson
mean:

$$
u_g \mid \lambda_g, \nu_c \sim \text{Poisson}(\nu_c \lambda_g).
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-thinned-poisson}

The thinned rate $\nu_c \lambda_g$ is itself Gamma-distributed, because scaling
a Gamma random variable by a constant only changes the rate parameter, not the
shape:

$$
\nu_c \lambda_g \sim \text{Gamma}(r_g, \; \beta / \nu_c).
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-thinned-gamma}

Marginalizing over the latent rate, the observed count follows

$$
u_g \sim \text{NB}(r_g, \hat{p}),
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-effective-nb}

where $\hat{p}$ is the effective success probability after capture (see
@eq-cap-phat-reminder), which depends on both $\nu_c$ and $\beta$ (equivalently,
on $\nu_c$ and $\phi$). The crucial observation is that **the shape parameter
$r_g$ is unchanged by thinning**---only the rate (or equivalently, the success
probability) is modified. This is a general property of the Poisson--Gamma
mixture: binomial thinning acts on the Poisson layer, which rescales the Gamma
rate but cannot alter the Gamma shape.

#### Exact invariance of the Dirichlet distribution

Since the effective negative binomial for the observed counts retains the
original shape parameters $\underline{r} = (r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_D)$, the
Dirichlet conditional for normalized expression remains

$$
\underline{\rho} \mid \underline{r} \sim \text{Dirichlet}(\underline{r}),
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-dirichlet-invariant}

regardless of the value of $\nu_c$. The capture probability enters the
likelihood only through the effective success probability $\hat{p}$, which
governs the *total count* distribution but not the *compositional* distribution.

Now consider what happens when the model adopts a misspecified capture
probability $\tilde{\nu}_c \neq \nu_c$. The observed data constrain the
effective NB parameters $(r_g, \hat{p}_c)$ for each gene and cell. As shown
above, $r_g$ does not depend on $\nu_c$---it is determined entirely by the
biological mean $\mu_g$ and the overdispersion structure. The model compensates
for the wrong $\tilde{\nu}_c$ by adjusting $\mu_g$ and $\phi$ (or $p$) to
maintain the same observed counts, but these adjustments are constrained to
preserve $r_g$:

$$
\tilde{r}_g = r_g, \quad \text{for all } g.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-r-invariant}

To see this explicitly, define the misspecification ratio $\kappa =
\nu_c / \tilde{\nu}_c$. The data constrain the expected observed count
$\langle u_{gc} \rangle = \nu_c \mu_g$, so the model must adopt
$\tilde{\mu}_g = \kappa \mu_g$ to compensate. Similarly, the data constrain
the effective success probability $\hat{p}_c$ (and hence the variance
structure), which forces $\tilde{\phi}$ to adjust in the opposite direction so
that the product entering $r_g$ remains unchanged. The details of this
compensation depend on the NB parameterization convention, but in every case the
$\kappa$ factors cancel:

$$
\tilde{r}_g = f(\tilde{\mu}_g, \tilde{\phi}) = f(\kappa \mu_g, \tilde{\phi}) =
r_g, $${#eq-diffexp-rob-r-cancel}

where $f(\mu, \phi)$ denotes the parameterization-specific formula relating
$r_g$ to $\mu_g$ and $\phi$. This cancellation is not a coincidence---it is a
necessary consequence of the thinning invariance @eq-diffexp-rob-thinned-gamma:
the data determine $r_g$ through the likelihood, and no amount of $\nu_c$
misspecification can change what the data require.

Since the Dirichlet parameters are exactly preserved, **the entire distribution
of normalized expression is identical** under the true and misspecified models.
Every quantity derived from this distribution---the Dirichlet mean proportions,
the Dirichlet variances and covariances, the logistic-normal approximation in
ALR space, and hence the CLR coordinates, their means, and their full covariance
structure---is exactly invariant.

#### Extension to gene-specific $p_g$ {#sec-diffexp-rob-hierarchical}

The preceding argument assumed the shared-$p$ model in which all genes have a
common success probability, so the compositional distribution is exactly
Dirichlet. Under the hierarchical model developed in @sec-hierarchical-p, each
gene has its own success probability $p_g$, and compositions are no longer
Dirichlet but instead follow the generalized Gamma-ratio distribution described
in @sec-hier-gamma-sampling. A natural concern is whether the exact invariance
result survives this generalization. It does, and the argument is
straightforward.

Recall from @eq-hier-composition-expanded that under the gene-specific model,
compositions are generated by drawing independent Gamma variates $\gamma_g \sim
\text{Gamma}(r_g, 1)$, scaling each by the gene-specific odds factor $1/\theta_g
= (1 - p_g)/p_g$, and normalizing:

$$
\rho_g = \frac{
    \gamma_g / \theta_g
}{
    \sum_{j=1}^G \gamma_j / \theta_j
}.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-hier-composition}

Under capture thinning with probability $\nu_c$, the Gamma rate for gene $g$
shifts from $\theta_g$ to $\theta_g / \nu_c$ (@eq-diffexp-rob-thinned-gamma),
while the shape $r_g$ is preserved. The effective odds factor for the thinned
model is therefore $\nu_c / \theta_g$. Substituting into the composition
formula:

$$
\rho_g^{\text{obs}} = \frac{ \gamma_g \cdot \nu_c / \theta_g }{ \sum_{j=1}^G
    \gamma_j \cdot \nu_c / \theta_j } = \frac{ \gamma_g / \theta_g }{
\sum_{j=1}^G \gamma_j / \theta_j } = \rho_g. $${#eq-diffexp-rob-hier-nuc-cancel}

The cell-level capture probability $\nu_c$ enters every gene's scaling factor
uniformly---it is a property of the cell, not the gene---and therefore cancels
in the ratio that defines the composition. This cancellation is exact and holds
for arbitrary $\nu_c$.

Under capture misspecification with ratio $\kappa = \nu_c / \tilde{\nu}_c$, the
model compensates by adjusting the gene-specific odds to $\tilde{\theta}_g =
\kappa \cdot \theta_g$ (since the data constrain the effective rate $\theta_g /
\nu_c$, and the model interprets this as $\tilde{\theta}_g / \tilde{\nu}_c$).
The composition under the misspecified model is

$$
\tilde{\rho}_g = \frac{
    \gamma_g / \tilde{\theta}_g
}{
    \sum_{j=1}^G \gamma_j / \tilde{\theta}_j
} = \frac{
    \gamma_g / (\kappa \theta_g)
}{
    \sum_{j=1}^G \gamma_j / (\kappa \theta_j)
} = \frac{
    \gamma_g / \theta_g
}{
    \sum_{j=1}^G \gamma_j / \theta_j
} = \rho_g.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-hier-kappa-cancel}

The uniform $\kappa$ factor cancels identically. Combined with the invariance of
the shape parameters ($\tilde{r}_g = r_g$, @eq-diffexp-rob-r-invariant), this
establishes that **the full compositional distribution is exactly invariant to
capture probability misspecification, even under the gene-specific $p_g$ model**.
The generalized Gamma-ratio composition inherits the same structural protection
as the Dirichlet special case, because the mechanism is the same in both:
capture probability is a cell-level quantity that scales all genes uniformly, and
normalization to proportions annihilates any uniform multiplicative factor.

#### Why the capture degeneracy cannot affect compositions {#sec-diffexp-rob-degeneracy}

The preceding subsections established the invariance result through the
Poisson--Gamma representation. A natural concern remains: the variable capture
model has a well-known identifiability issue (see @sec-cap-identifiability),
where the observed library size constrains only the product $\nu_c \cdot \mu_T$
and cannot separately determine the capture probability $\nu_c$ and the
biological total $\mu_T$. If the model has a genuine degeneracy, how can we
claim that anything---even compositions---is immune to it? The answer is that the
degeneracy lives in a subspace of the parameter space that is structurally
orthogonal to the compositional distribution. This subsection makes this
statement precise.

##### What the data identify without ambiguity

Consider first the canonical NB parameterization, where the parameters are the
shape $r_g$ and the success probability $p$ (or $p_g$ in the gene-specific
model). After capture thinning, the observed counts for gene $g$ in cell $c$
follow $u_{gc} \sim \text{NB}(r_g, \hat{p}_c)$, where $\hat{p}_c$ is the
effective success probability that absorbs the capture efficiency. The
log-likelihood of a single observation $k$ from $\text{NB}(r, \hat{p})$ is

$$
\ell(r, \hat{p}; k) = \log\Gamma(k + r) - \log\Gamma(r) - \log k!
+ r \log \hat{p} + k \log(1 - \hat{p}). $${#eq-diffexp-rob-nb-loglik}

The shape $r$ enters through the $\log\Gamma(k + r) - \log\Gamma(r)$ terms,
which control the overdispersion structure of the count distribution. The
effective success probability $\hat{p}$ enters through $r \log \hat{p} + k
\log(1 - \hat{p})$, which controls the mean-to-variance ratio. These two
parameters affect the likelihood in fundamentally different ways: given
sufficient data, both $r_g$ and $\hat{p}_c$ are **uniquely identified**. The
variance-to-mean ratio of the observed counts determines $\hat{p}_c$ (since
$\text{Var}(u_{gc}) / \langle u_{gc} \rangle = 1/\hat{p}_c$), and the mean then
pins down $r_g$ (since $\langle u_{gc} \rangle = r_g(1 - \hat{p}_c)/\hat{p}_c$).
There is no degeneracy in the pair $(r_g, \hat{p}_c)$.

##### Where the degeneracy lives

The degeneracy appears only when one attempts to decompose the effective
parameters into their biological and technical components. The effective success
probability is a function of the biological $p$ (or $p_g$) and the capture
probability $\nu_c$:

$$
\hat{p}_c = \frac{p}{p + \nu_c(1 - p)},
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-phat-decomposition}

or equivalently, in terms of the odds $\theta = p/(1-p)$:

$$
\hat{\theta}_c = \frac{\theta}{\nu_c}.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-thetahat-decomposition}

Many pairs $(\theta, \nu_c)$ produce the same effective odds $\hat{\theta}_c$.
This is the $\nu_c$-$\mu_T$ degeneracy: a one-dimensional curve in the $(\theta,
\nu_c)$ plane along which the observed data are identical. In the gene-specific
model, the same structure holds gene by gene: the data identify
$\hat{\theta}_{gc} = \theta_g / \nu_c$ for each gene, but the decomposition of
$\hat{\theta}_{gc}$ into $\theta_g$ and $\nu_c$ is degenerate.

The crucial structural observation is that **this degeneracy is always a uniform
rescaling across genes**. Because $\nu_c$ is a cell-level parameter---it
describes the efficiency of mRNA capture for the entire cell, not for individual
genes---the degenerate direction takes the form

$$
\theta_g \to \alpha \, \theta_g, \quad
\nu_c \to \alpha \, \nu_c, \quad
\text{for all } g \text{ simultaneously},
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-degen-direction}

where $\alpha > 0$ is a single scalar that shifts all genes by the same
multiplicative factor. A gene-specific rescaling $\theta_g \to \alpha_g \,
\theta_g$ (with $\alpha_g$ varying across genes) would change the effective odds
$\hat{\theta}_{gc}$ for that gene, contradicting what the data require.

##### Why uniform degeneracies are invisible to compositions

Compositions are defined as ratios of contributions:

$$
\rho_g = \frac{\gamma_g / \theta_g}{\sum_{j} \gamma_j / \theta_j},
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-comp-ratio}

where $\gamma_g \sim \text{Gamma}(r_g, 1)$ are independent draws. If the model
sits at a different point on the degenerate manifold---with $\tilde{\theta}_g =
\alpha\,\theta_g$ for all $g$---the composition becomes

$$
\tilde{\rho}_g = \frac{\gamma_g / (\alpha\,\theta_g)}{\sum_{j}
\gamma_j / (\alpha\,\theta_j)} = \frac{\gamma_g / \theta_g}{\sum_{j}
\gamma_j / \theta_j} = \rho_g.
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-uniform-cancel}

The uniform factor $1/\alpha$ appears in every term of both the numerator and
denominator and cancels identically. This is not an approximation---it is an
algebraic identity. Compositions are, by construction, insensitive to the
overall scale of the rate parameters. Since the capture degeneracy can only move
the model along a direction that rescales all $\theta_g$ uniformly, and since
compositions annihilate uniform rescalings, the degeneracy is structurally
invisible to the compositional distribution.

##### The complete picture

Putting the pieces together:

1. **Shape parameters $r_g$**: uniquely identified by the data. No degeneracy,
   no ambiguity. These are directly constrained by the overdispersion structure
   of the observed counts through the NB likelihood
   (@eq-diffexp-rob-nb-loglik).

2. **Effective success probabilities $\hat{p}_c$ (or $\hat{\theta}_{gc}$)**:
   uniquely identified by the data. These determine the mean-to-variance ratio
   of the observed counts.

3. **Decomposition $(\theta_g, \nu_c)$**: degenerate. The data constrain only
   the ratio $\hat{\theta}_{gc} = \theta_g / \nu_c$. But the degenerate
   direction is always a uniform rescaling across genes
   (@eq-diffexp-rob-degen-direction), because $\nu_c$ is a cell-level quantity.

4. **Compositions**: depend on $\underline{r}$ (no degeneracy) and on the
   $\theta_g$ values only through ratios that cancel uniform scalings
   (@eq-diffexp-rob-uniform-cancel). The degenerate subspace is therefore
   orthogonal to the compositional distribution.

The robustness of CLR inference does not require the capture degeneracy to be
resolved. The biology-informed prior on total mRNA (@sec-capture-prior) is
valuable for anchoring the biological-level parameters $\mu_g$ and $\phi$ (or
$p_g$), and hence for the biological metrics discussed later. But for
compositional inference, the CLR results are **exact regardless of where the
posterior sits on the degenerate manifold**. This is what makes the robustness
result a structural property of the model rather than a consequence of having a
good prior.

#### Consequences for CLR-based differential expression

The invariance of the compositional distribution---whether Dirichlet under the
shared-$p$ model or the generalized Gamma-ratio under the gene-specific $p_g$
model (@sec-diffexp-rob-hierarchical)---has immediate and powerful consequences
for the differential expression framework developed in @sec-diffexp through
@sec-diffexp-eb:

- **CLR means**: exactly invariant, since CLR coordinates are a deterministic
  function of the Dirichlet parameters.

- **CLR covariances**: exactly invariant, since the posterior predictive
  covariance of the logistic-normal approximation (@sec-normalization) depends
  only on the Dirichlet parameters $\underline{r}$.

- **CLR differences $\Delta_g$**: exactly invariant between conditions, since
  each condition's CLR distribution is independently invariant.

- **Per-gene z-scores, lfsr, and tail probabilities**: exactly invariant, since
  both the means and variances of $\Delta_g$ are preserved.

- **PEFP thresholds and gene rankings**: exactly invariant, since the lfsr
  values that enter the PEFP machinery (@sec-diffexp-pefp) are unchanged.

- **Gene-set contrasts and ILR balances**: exactly invariant, since these are
  linear functions of the CLR coordinates.

In short, **every quantity in the compositional DE pipeline is perfectly
robust to capture probability misspecification**. This holds under both the
shared-$p$ model and the gene-specific $p_g$ generalization. It is not an
approximate result that holds only for small perturbations or in the
large-$r_T$ limit---it is an exact structural property of the model.

#### Contrast with biological-level metrics

The invariance that protects CLR inference does **not** extend to the
biological-level metrics developed earlier in this section. While the shape
parameters $r_g$ are invariant (and, in the gene-specific model, the
$\theta_g$-dependent composition is shielded by the uniform cancellation of
$\nu_c$), the biological parameters $\mu_g$ and $\phi$ (or $p_g$) individually
are not: the model compensates for a wrong $\nu_c$ by shifting these parameters,
and the biological metrics inherit those shifts.

**Biological LFC** (@eq-diffexp-bio-lfc). The biological log-fold change is
$\text{LFC}_g = \log(\mu_g^A / \mu_g^B)$. Under misspecification with
condition-specific ratios $\kappa^A$ and $\kappa^B$, the inferred means become
$\tilde{\mu}_g^A = \kappa^A \cdot \mu_g^A$ and
$\tilde{\mu}_g^B = \kappa^B \cdot \mu_g^B$, giving

$$
\widetilde{\text{LFC}}_g = \log\!\left(\frac{\kappa^A \cdot \mu_g^A} {\kappa^B
\cdot \mu_g^B}\right) = \text{LFC}_g +
\log\!\left(\frac{\kappa^A}{\kappa^B}\right). $${#eq-diffexp-rob-lfc-bias}

Every gene's LFC is shifted by the same additive offset $\log(\kappa^A /
\kappa^B)$. The *sign* and *ranking* of LFC values are preserved (since the
offset is gene-independent), but the *absolute magnitudes* are biased. This
means that practical significance thresholds $|\text{LFC}_g| > \tau$ will
include or exclude different genes depending on the direction and size of the
offset.

**Log-variance ratio** (@eq-diffexp-bio-lvr). The LVR inherits the same additive
bias as the LFC and picks up an additional term from the change in $\phi$. Both
are gene-independent, so rankings are again preserved but absolute values are
shifted.

**Jeffreys divergence** (@eq-diffexp-bio-jeffreys). The Gamma rate parameter
$\beta_g$ absorbs the capture scaling nonlinearly. While the divergence is not
simply shifted by an additive offset, it is systematically inflated or deflated
for all genes, again distorting absolute magnitudes without necessarily changing
rankings.

This contrast reinforces the decision framework in @sec-diffexp-bio-decision:
**use CLR as the primary screen** (where inference is exactly invariant to
capture misspecification), then **validate with biological metrics** (where
absolute values should be interpreted cautiously when the capture model is
uncertain).

#### The mixture model case

A particularly important setting is a **mixture model** where different cell
types (components) are jointly modeled. Different cell types may have genuinely
different total mRNA contents $M_c$, so a single $M_0$ cannot accurately anchor
all components simultaneously. In practice, this manifests as different mixture
components converging to different effective capture probability scalings.

The thinning-invariance argument applies **per-component**: within each mixture
component $k$, the shape parameters $r_g^{(k)}$ are determined by the data and
are invariant to the capture probability assigned to that component. The CLR
difference between conditions A and B within the same component,

$$
\Delta_g^{(k)} = z_{\text{CLR},g}^{A,(k)} - z_{\text{CLR},g}^{B,(k)},
$${#eq-diffexp-rob-mixture-delta}

is therefore exactly preserved even when different components learn different
effective capture scalings. Under the shared-$p$ model, the key requirement is
that the shared structure holds *within* each component---which is guaranteed by
the model architecture---not that the capture scaling be the same *across*
components. Under the gene-specific $p_g$ model, even this requirement is
unnecessary: as shown in @sec-diffexp-rob-hierarchical, the uniform
cancellation of $\nu_c$ in the composition ratio protects the result regardless
of how the $p_g$ vary across genes.

This property is especially reassuring for mixture models where the
biology-informed prior on $M_0$ may conflict with the true total mRNA of one or
more cell types. Even if the $M_0$ anchor is imperfect and the model compensates
by adjusting the effective capture scaling differently for each component, the
CLR-based differential expression analysis within each component remains
perfectly valid. The biological-level metrics, by contrast, would reflect the
component-specific scaling errors and require careful interpretation.
